topic: JVM Internals (Memory Model, G1/ZGC, Class Loading)

questions.scenario: You are debugging a production issue where a high-throughput Java 17 microservice recently switched its Garbage Collector from `G1` to `ZGC`. While the P99 latency has dropped to sub-millisecond levels, the application's overall throughput has decreased by 15%, and the CPU utilization has increased by 20%. The heap size is 64GB.
questions.question: Which characteristic of the `ZGC` implementation in Java 17 most likely explains this trade-off?
options: A) ZGC performs all compacting work in Stop-The-World (STW) pauses, causing CPU spikes.
B) The lack of a 'Generational' design in Java 17 ZGC requires the collector to scan the entire heap more frequently, increasing CPU overhead.
C) ZGC uses 'Store Barriers' which are significantly more expensive than the 'Load Barriers' used by G1.
D) ZGC requires 'Thread Local Allocation Buffers' (TLABs) to be disabled, leading to contention in the shared heap space.
questions.correct_answer_text: B) The lack of a 'Generational' design in Java 17 ZGC requires the collector to scan the entire heap more frequently, increasing CPU overhead.
questions.explanation: In Java 17, `ZGC` is non-generational. Unlike `G1`, which divides the heap into young and old generations to exploit the weak generational hypothesis (most objects die young), non-generational `ZGC` must process the entire heap to reclaim memory. This results in higher CPU consumption and lower throughput because the collector spends more cycles tracking long-lived objects alongside short-lived ones. Generational `ZGC` was only introduced later in JDK 21 to address this specific overhead.

questions.question: Why does the `saveRecord` method fail to start a new independent transaction, instead joining the existing transaction or running without one?
options: A) The JVM ClassLoader delegates the `AuditService` to the Bootstrap ClassLoader, bypassing the Spring Proxy.
B) Spring's `@Transactional` annotation is not compatible with the `REQUIRES_NEW` propagation when using `CGLIB` proxies.
C) The internal call to `saveRecord` bypasses the Spring AOP proxy, meaning the interceptor logic for `Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW` is never executed.
D) The `G1` Garbage Collector reclaims the transaction metadata because it is stored in the 'Survivor' space.
questions.correct_answer_text: C) The internal call to `saveRecord` bypasses the Spring AOP proxy, meaning the interceptor logic for `Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW` is never executed.
questions.explanation: Spring `@Transactional` works via AOP proxies. When one method calls another method within the same class (self-invocation), the call is made on the `this` reference rather than the proxy object. Consequently, the Spring interceptor that handles the `Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW` logic is never invoked. To fix this, `saveRecord` should be in a separate service or the bean should be self-injected (though the latter is a code smell).

questions.scenario: You are debugging a production issue where a modular application server throws a `java.lang.LinkageError` with the message: 'loader constraint violation: loader ... wants to load class com.util.Parser, but that class has already been loaded by a different loader'. This occurs when a plugin attempts to pass a `Parser` object to the core system.
questions.question: What fundamental JVM Class Loading principle is being violated here?
options: A) The Visibility Principle: A child ClassLoader cannot see classes loaded by a parent ClassLoader.
B) The Uniqueness Principle: A class is uniquely identified by its fully qualified name AND its defining ClassLoader instance.
C) The Delegation Principle: The Bootstrap ClassLoader must always load classes from the 'ext' directory before the 'app' directory.
D) The Garbage Collection Principle: Classes cannot be unloaded if their ClassLoader is still reachable.
questions.correct_answer_text: B) The Uniqueness Principle: A class is uniquely identified by its fully qualified name AND its defining ClassLoader instance.
questions.explanation: In the JVM, the identity of a class is defined by the pair of its fully qualified name and the instance of the `ClassLoader` that loaded it. If `ClassLoaderA` and `ClassLoaderB` both load `com.util.Parser`, the JVM treats them as two completely different and incompatible types. Passing an instance of one to a method expecting the other results in a `LinkageError` or `ClassCastException` because the types do not match in the JVM's internal runtime constant pool.
